title: Making a leather bag strap with a swivel snap hook
subtitle: Cut, edge, and burnish a simple leather shoulder strap with riveted hardware ends
excerpt: How to make a simple veg-tan leather bag strap with a swivel snap hook at each end. Covers cutting the strap, bevelling and burnishing the edges, punching holes for rivets, and setting the copper or brass rivets to attach the hardware.
sourceNotes: The Leatherworking Handbook, Valerie Michael (2010). Al Stohlman, The Art of Hand Sewing Leather (reference format). City and Guilds Level 2 Leatherwork (UK). Tandy Leather project guides (publicly available reference).

glossaryTerms.definition: A metal hook with a rotating (swivel) body and a spring-loaded gate, used to attach a bag strap to a bag's D-ring or ring fitting. The swivel allows the strap to rotate without twisting. Sold in nickel, brass, and antique brass finishes; common sizes are 20 mm and 25 mm gate width (matching the strap width). Not the same as a trigger snap, which has a trigger-release gate rather than a spring gate.

A two-part fastener consisting of a post and a cap, set through a drilled or punched hole. The post is passed through the leather and the hardware, the cap is placed over the post, and both are hammered together with a rivet setter tool. Copper rivets are traditional for leather strap hardware; they work-harden as they are set and do not loosen over time. Size is stated as post diameter (3 mm or 4 mm for straps) and post length to suit the material thickness.

content.text: A simple leather bag strap uses four components: the leather strap, two
, two D-rings or small rings to thread the leather through, and

to secure the folded ends. No stitching is required. The whole strap can be made in an hour with basic leatherwork tools. Use 3 to 4 mm veg-tan leather for strength; thinner veg-tan is too flexible for a strap carrying any load.

A strip of 3 to 4 mm veg-tan leather, 25 mm wide and long enough for the strap length plus 100 mm for the two folded ends. Two 25 mm swivel snap hooks and two 25 mm D-rings (or sliding rings). Four copper rivets (3 mm post diameter, 10 mm post length). A hole punch or single-hole punch to fit the rivet post diameter. A rivet setter and a small hammer. The leatherwork knife and cutting mat. The edge beveller (size 2). The leather burnisher and a small bowl of water.

Mark the strap length on the leather using a metal ruler and the leatherwork knife; place the ruler on the face side and score a firm single pass rather than sawing. Cut to width against the ruler. The cut edges should be clean and straight. Mark and cut the strap length with 50 mm extra at each end for the folded hardware loops.
Step 2, bevel and burnish the edges

Run the edge beveller along all four edges of the strap (both faces, both long sides, and both ends) removing the sharp right-angle corner and leaving a chamfered edge. Dampen the edges lightly with water. Run the leather burnisher back and forth along each edge with firm pressure: friction from the burnisher polishes the cut fibre into a sealed, smooth edge. Work each edge in a single continuous direction, repeating passes until the edge turns smooth and slightly darker.
Step 3, thread the hardware and fold the ends

Thread each end of the strap through a D-ring and fold 50 mm of the strap back against itself; the D-ring is captured in the fold. The D-ring will carry the swivel snap hook at each end. Thread the snap hook through the D-ring. Hold the folded end firmly and mark the rivet positions: two rivets per end, spaced evenly, centred on the fold width.
Step 4, punch holes and set the rivets

Punch through both layers of leather at each rivet mark with the hole punch. Insert the rivet post through from the face side. Place the cap over the post on the back. Place the rivet setter over the cap and strike it firmly with the hammer in two or three sharp blows until the rivet head is fully collapsed and the two layers of leather are pulled together without a gap. Do not strike so hard that the rivet deforms; one sharp tap positions the setter, one firmer tap sets the rivet.

attrs.symptom: Rivet cap deforming rather than collapsing cleanly
attrs.cause: Rivet setter not centred on the cap before striking.
attrs.fix: Use a larger dome setter if the cap is deforming outward. Centre the setter cup over the rivet cap and keep the hammer blow vertical.

attrs.symptom: Cut edge of the strap crumbling or fraying
attrs.cause: Leather cut with a blunt knife or in multiple passes.
attrs.fix: A clean leatherwork cut is made in one firm pass with a sharp blade. Replace the blade and re-cut if the edge is ragged. Burnish the new edge more firmly.
